cot:
The opponent led Grimmsnarl and Calyrex-Ice, and my T1 sequence leaves my Calyrex-Shadow at +2 SpA while their Calyrex-Ice protected to dodge my Taunt and Grimmsnarl set Light Screen. Calyrex-Ice threatens to set Trick Room this turn, which would give them devastating board control. However, at +2 SpA, Calyrex-Shadow is extraordinarily threatening even through Light Screen.

A +2 Astral Barrage deals massive damage, and if Calyrex-Ice stays Psychic/Ice, it takes 169-200% from a Spell Tag-boosted Astral Barrage, guaranteeing an OHKO through Light Screen. The opponent's main counterplay to this is to Terastallize Calyrex-Ice into a resist or neutral typing, such as Water or Fire, so it can survive the hit and set Trick Room. If it Teras Water, +2 Astral Barrage (assuming spread damage and Light Screen) drops to around 46-55% damage. 

I could attempt to Taunt Calyrex-Ice again, but since it is very likely holding Clear Amulet (57% usage), using Knock Off on that slot is highly advantageous. If it Teras and sets Trick Room, I at least strip its Clear Amulet, ensuring my Intimidate cycling (like bringing Incineroar in and out) will actually function to blunt its Glacial Lances under Trick Room. If it doesn't Tera, Astral Barrage OHKOs it anyway, and Knock Off catches whoever comes into that slot. Knock Off also chips Calyrex-Ice meaningfully in case my Astral Barrage rolls low (if I lack a damage-boosting item) or it Terastallizes, putting it in range of my priority or subsequent attacks. Astral Barrage pressures both slots heavily.

cot:
With Trick Room active, the opposing base 50s (Calyrex-Ice and Ursaluna) will move before Incineroar (base 60) and my other faster pieces. Iron Treads (37% HP) is virtually guaranteed to faint to any attack in its slot, including a Glacial Lance. Hard switching Iron Treads to Urshifu-Rapid-Strike preserves it while Urshifu absorbs Glacial Lance beautifully (resists, taking only ~16-19% from -1 Calyrex).

Ursaluna just entered the field and hasn't activated its Flame Orb yet. It is extremely common for Ursaluna to Protect on this turn to safely proc Guts. 
- If Ursaluna Protects: Incineroar easily tanks a -1 Glacial Lance and then safely U-turns out to bring in Sinistcha, keeping momentum.
- If Ursaluna attacks instead of Protecting: It will likely aim a Ground move (Headlong Rush/Earthquake) at Incineroar, which will KO it, or at the Treads slot (which Urshifu can tank). If Incineroar faints before U-turning, it achieves the same result—a clean switch to a fresh Pokémon, letting me reposition for the next turn.

U-turning into Calyrex-Ice rather than Ursaluna ensures the U-turn connects even if Ursaluna Protects, granting me the pivot. Urshifu taking the Treads slot covers the Glacial Lance and any incoming attacks better than sacrificing Treads for nothing, while Incineroar's U-turn guarantees a safe board state going into turn 4 regardless of Ursaluna's action.

cot:
Turn 1 revealed the absolute speed order of the field: Ting-Lu used Protect, then Kyogre moved, followed by Calyrex-Shadow, and finally Miraidon used Snarl. Because my bulky Calyrex (invested in HP/SpD) outsped Miraidon, we know this Miraidon is exceptionally slow and bulky, while my Kyogre is the fastest on the field (likely Choice Scarfed). 

Since both Kyogre and Calyrex outspeed Miraidon, we have a guaranteed execution window. The threat matrix confirms that at -1 SpA, our combined spread damage (15.5% min from Water Spout + 25.1% min from Astral Barrage = 40.6%) strictly exceeds Miraidon's remaining 37% HP. This guarantees Miraidon faints before it can move, preventing another Snarl or any offensive pressure.

Ting-Lu Protected last turn, making a consecutive Protect highly likely to fail. It currently sits at 100% HP, but Kyogre's Water Spout in Rain threatens a massive 61.8%–130.4% damage even at -1 SpA, putting Ting-Lu in immediate danger of a KO or being left heavily crippled. There is no reason to pivot; maximizing our spread damage bypasses potential redirection, guarantees the Miraidon KO, and severely punishes Ting-Lu or any switch-ins.

  Spread 1 (42%): Modest  4 HP / 252 SpA / 252 Spe

[Weezing-Galar]
  Spread 1 (26%): Calm  252 HP / 236 SpD / 20 Spe
  Spread 2 (11%): Calm  252 HP / 4 Def / 252 SpD
  Moves (top): Protect 89% · Taunt 59% · Will-O-Wisp 45% · Poison Gas 35%
  Item: Sitrus Berry 33% (Covert Cloak 27%) | Ability: Neutralizing Gas 95% | Tera: Water 56%
cot:
The opponent leads Typhlosion-Hisui and Weezing-Galar. Weezing-Galar's Neutralizing Gas immediately suppresses both Whimsicott's Prankster and Terapagos's Tera Shell, fundamentally disrupting my turn 1 plan. With Prankster suppressed, Whimsicott is forced to use its base 116 speed. Typhlosion-Hisui is highly likely to be holding a Choice Scarf (61% usage), hitting effectively 220-241 speed, meaning it will outspeed Whimsicott and cleanly OHKO it with Eruption before it can set Tailwind or act. Leaving Whimsicott in is practically guaranteeing a free KO for the opponent.

To mitigate this, I need to pivot Whimsicott out. Zacian-Crowned is a valuable switch-in here for an extremely specific mechanical reason: Neutralizing Gas suppresses Intrepid Sword upon entry. However, Gen 9 mechanics dictate that if Weezing-Galar faints or leaves the field later, Intrepid Sword will trigger immediately at that moment. By bringing Zacian in now, I accept the damage from Eruption (which it survives if Typhlosion doesn't max SpA, or if Terapagos puts pressure on the slot), but more importantly, I position it to gain its +1 Attack exactly when I want it—once the gas clears.

For Terapagos, its Tera Shell is inactive, meaning it cannot sponge hits as safely as it normally would. However, we have an opportunity to capitalize on the board state. If I Terastallize Terapagos now, I consume my Tera early on a turn where I don't have speed control, leaving me vulnerable. Instead, I'll use a raw Tera Starstorm. Because I'm not Terastallizing, Tera Starstorm remains a Normal-type move. It is completely immune against the Ghost-type Typhlosion-Hisui, meaning the attack will effectively function as a single-target nuke aimed entirely at Weezing-Galar. Weezing is the linchpin of their strategy, suppressing abilities. Damaging or KOing Weezing removes the Neutralizing Gas, simultaneously reactivating my team's abilities—most notably granting Zacian-Crowned its delayed Intrepid Sword boost. This positioning optimizes my action economy and sets up a dominant turn 2.

cot:
The opponent has no backline left, meaning our win condition is to simply outlast Koraidon and Ursaluna. Sun is up for 3 more turns. Koraidon and Ursaluna both used Protect last turn, meaning they are virtually guaranteed to act this turn unless they risk a 30% double-Protect. 

Ursaluna is immensely threatening, with Facade capable of severely damaging Calyrex-Ice or even OHKOing Ho-Oh depending on investment. Koraidon, however, is heavily walled by Tera Water Calyrex-Ice and struggles to push massive damage into Ho-Oh with its STABs since Ho-Oh resists Flare Blitz and Collision Course. 

Given our current HP (Ho-Oh at 96%, Calyrex-Ice at 100%), if we simply commit to heavy healing and stalling this turn, we maximize our odds of weathering their strongest coordinated assault. Life Dew on Ho-Oh ensures that if Koraidon chips either of my Pokémon before Ursaluna attacks, the damage is immediately mitigated, keeping both out of KO range from a potential Facade or Flare Blitz. Calyrex-Ice using Leech Seed initiates passive recovery and chip damage against the opponent (specifically Ursaluna, mitigating its Guts-boosted output over time). 

Since we can afford to play defensively against an opposing board that has no immediate OHKOes on our Tera Water Calyrex-Ice from full (and Ho-Oh at full), this completely shuts down any variance or high-roll double targets they might attempt, locking in our endgame advantage.
